channel.title: NPR Topics: News
channel.description: NPR news, audio, and podcasts. Coverage of breaking stories, national and world news, politics, business, science, technology, and extended coverage of major national and world events.
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

item.title: How a single decision made a century ago split a family in half by race
item.description: Pope Leo's Black family roots inspired journalist Susan Saulny to research her Creole great-uncle who moved to Chicago, became white and didn't return. She describes her journey to reunite her family.
item.content:encoded: Pope Leo's Black family roots inspired journalist Susan Saulny to research her Creole great-uncle who moved to Chicago, became white and didn't return. She describes her journey to reunite her family.
item.dc:creator: Tonya Mosley

item.title: Greetings from Porto, whose lanes are lined with colorful textiles
item.description: Linen scarves, cotton aprons and dishtowels adorn the entrances to souvenir shops, many of which are run by Bangladeshis whose home country shares Portugal's rich tradition of textile manufacturing.
item.content:encoded: Linen scarves, cotton aprons and dishtowels adorn the entrances to souvenir shops, many of which are run by Bangladeshis whose home country shares Portugal's rich tradition of textile manufacturing.
item.dc:creator: Esme Nicholson

item.title: Takeaways from Iowa's primaries. And, DOJ nixes Trump's 'anti-weaponization' fund
item.description: Polls have now closed in six states that held primary elections yesterday. Here are the key takeaways. And, the Justice Department has scrapped plans for Trump's "anti-weaponization" fund.
item.content:encoded:
Polls have now closed in six states that held primary elections yesterday. Here are the key takeaways. And, the Justice Department has scrapped plans for Trump's "anti-weaponization" fund.

(Image credit: Charlie Neibergall)
item.dc:creator: Brittney Melton

item.title: President Trump seeks control of science funding
item.description: The White House Office of Management and Budget is moving to take more control of billions of dollars in federal grants. Critics say the proposed change would jeopardize the integrity of U.S. science.
item.content:encoded:
The White House Office of Management and Budget is moving to take more control of billions of dollars in federal grants. Critics say the proposed change would jeopardize the integrity of U.S. science.

(Image credit: Andrew Harnik)
item.dc:creator: Katia Riddle

item.title: Ukrainian drones strike a St. Petersburg oil terminal ahead of Putin visit
item.description: Ukrainian drones struck an oil terminal in St. Petersburg and set it ablaze, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy said, as the city hosted an annual economic forum promoted by President Putin.
item.content:encoded:
Ukrainian drones struck an oil terminal in St. Petersburg and set it ablaze, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy said, as the city hosted an annual economic forum promoted by President Putin.

(Image credit: AP Photo)
item.dc:creator: The Associated Press

item.title: Thieves are targeting the world's copper. This phone company is fighting back
item.description: The value of copper is rising, and thieves can make money by stripping it from phone poles, streetlights and EV chargers. But those thefts cost the rest of us.
item.content:encoded:
The value of copper is rising, and thieves can make money by stripping it from phone poles, streetlights and EV chargers. But those thefts cost the rest of us.

(Image credit: John Ruwitch/NPR)
item.dc:creator: John Ruwitch

item.title: The White House's new site about 'aliens' has nothing to do with UFOs
item.description: The site compares undocumented immigrants to extraterrestrials, refers to people as "it," and says "they do not belong here."
item.content:encoded:
The site compares undocumented immigrants to extraterrestrials, refers to people as "it," and says "they do not belong here."

(Image credit: The White House)
item.dc:creator: Huo Jingnan

item.title: We finally know enough about how the brain breaks to focus on fixing it, experts say
item.description: The Allen Institute in Seattle says scientists have now learned enough about how the brain works to start fixing it when it breaks.
item.content:encoded:
The Allen Institute in Seattle says scientists have now learned enough about how the brain works to start fixing it when it breaks.

(Image credit: Erik Dinnel)
item.dc:creator: Jon Hamilton

item.title: Trump administration shares new work requirements for Medicaid recipients
item.description: The Trump administration this week released guidance for states on the work rules many Americans on Medicaid will need to abide by in order to be eligible for benefits on Jan. 1.
item.content:encoded: The Trump administration this week released guidance for states on the work rules many Americans on Medicaid will need to abide by in order to be eligible for benefits on Jan. 1.
item.dc:creator: Julie Rovner

item.title: Sen. Warner warns of risks from Pulte's intelligence appointment
item.description: NPR's Steve Inskeep asks Sen. Mark Warner of Virginia, the top Democrat on the Senate Intelligence committee, about mortgage chief Bill Pulte's move to acting director of national intelligence.
item.content:encoded:
NPR's Steve Inskeep asks Sen. Mark Warner of Virginia, the top Democrat on the Senate Intelligence committee, about mortgage chief Bill Pulte's move to acting director of national intelligence.

(Image credit: Kevin Dietsch)
item.dc:creator: Steve Inskeep
